replies.author: @500dollarjapanesetoaster8
replies.text:
A gentle reminder that they spent $4 billion just to get the rights to it, not to mention all of the costs of producing the films. Disney claims they've made $12 billion total on Star Wars across film, merch, etc. The cost to produce 3 main films (TROS, TLJ, TFA) alone was 1.5 bil, which does not include all of the marketing budgets. Very hard to find exact data, but at least 500 mil across those 3. From what I could find, the gross for those 3 movies was $4.4 bil. Respectable, but I don't think the other series, projects, etc. have come anywhere close to those sort of returns (Baby Yoda dolls aside). There is also expense of financing such vast amounts. There was the 400 mil Star Wars hotel that was vastly overpriced and flopped.
The bigger issue is that all fan enthusiasm for spending like they did for the first 3 Disney SW films is no longer there, so future revenue has been essentially sabotaged.
tl;dr Disney probably has turned out a profit from this 10+ yr debacle (but not as much as they've claimed), but their future profits are very much in doubt.

replies.author: @jjc5407
replies.text:
@CrimsonSunriseProductions were the audiobooks edited?  The novels are a disjointed mess.

Troy Denning inevitably creates new "powers" because that's what he does.  He loved to prefix every instance of the Force being used with the word "Force" even though it was rarely necessary.  We know a Jedi can leap 20 feet in the air, we don't need to be told he or she "Force leapt".  I detested his "video game" short-hand.  It was lazy,, undescriptive language.  Having characters use those terms was even more jarring.  When did you ever hear a character in the films saying something like "use Force-telekinesis"?  And as for "flow walking" that goes way beyond what was established in the films.  He's one of those authors that can't help trying to outdo George Lucas and make his characters more powerful.

Then there was Karen Traviss who clearly wanted to write a tedious trilogy about Boba Fett and Mandalorians and thought she was Tolkien-esque by making up a language for them.  Her instalments are like reading a separate story altogether while we wait for the main storylines to resume.

Both those two seemed to be writing largely different stories with only minimal crossover.  Only Aaron Allston made an effort to contribute to a coherent series.

Each authors had specific characters that were "theirs".  Allston kept his X-Wing characters appearances to a minimum, but Denning got Alema Rar and Lumiya, the latter being a character resurrected from the comics as she'd never previously been mentioned in the novels, and Traviss monopolised Fett and the Mandos.  As a result characters would disappear or virtually disappear from the series for an entire novel or two before reappearing.

Meanwhile the whole Jacen Solo story was just a rehash of Vader's.  His co-Chief of State of the "Galactic Alliance" is someone who never would believably have been accepted given her past history.

Luke teaches Jaina about the shatterpoint in the, but the author completely misunderstands Stover's original concept and then it never even comes into play anyway.

Luke also acts out of revenge after a character's death but it's OK because somehow that's allowed even though it's not the Jedi way.

This is then used as an excuse to neuter Luke.  Instead of ending Jacen's reign of terror when he has the chance he refuses to act because he claims it would cause him to fall to the Dark Side.  Instead he lets Jacen kill tens of millions.  That's beyond pathetic.

All in all it was a dreadful series of 9 books.  Somehow they managed to trump it with the Fate of the Jedi series which was even worse.

replies.text: @starlit_hawser11 I kind of agree and disagree. The prequels were crap for the most part. But they felt like Star Wars. Kenobi was cool and pretty good . So was Yoda. replies.text: @UpFront-A Well the fixes are simple, it should have been framed differently. First one Anakin is in his 20s getting trained, clone wars break out, action and so on. Third one is the tie up with Vader hunting down and killing the Jedi as was mentioned in the original film. Instead of a few brief scenes of the clones doing it in Revenge of the Sith. Kenobi going into hiding with Luke and Leia going to Bail etc Yoda in exile. That is how it should have been done and that way you dont have the whole first film wasted on kid Anakin. And no whiney brat version in the other two. It was vital to get the casting right and Haden was the wrong choice, so was Portman. Soooo wooden and then add in the poor script and its all a mess, apart from bits of the third movie. There is a lot to like in the three films but far too much that is bad. replies.author: @TheChristianPsychopath
replies.text:
@GabrielAlcala956 No, not even close.  I was in Star Wars groups when prequels came out.  Sure we poked at their flaws, but there was never a sense that Lucasfilm truly hated the fandom.  There was a lot of genuine excitement for expanding the story, exploring a new time period, genuine love for Qi-Gon and Obi-wan.  I still have an extensive Ep I Amidala collection.  The gaslighting that went on with the sequels was extreme and abusive.

But mostly it's the kids factor.  Lucas was right.  The kids liked Jar Jar, more importantly the kids liked the films in general.  TCW is flawed but that fandom left a lot of people deeply invested.

The kids are not into the sequels.  They made some violent and bleak PG-13 films that were morally confused and had nothing to offer children aside from roasted Porgs.  The kids do like Ahsoka from TCW and Rebels and Grogu.  I went to a teens graduation party this past year.  Ahsoka and Grogu, even Ewoks, but nothing from the sequels.

Also the sequels made the prequels look like Shakespeare by comparison.  There were execution issues, but there was a good story there... and it gets clearer what Lucas was trying to do if you read the novels.

The sequels had no plan, and no clear moral or point to them.  Just a confused jumble of directors who didn't understand the themes of the franchise.

Also we HAVE better sequels. The Heir to the Empire trilogy is excellent and we've had that since 1991.  Legacy of the Force is a dumpster fire of out of character decisions to make the plot happen, but it's still a better story of Han and Leia's son falling to the dark side.  It's Tolstoy next to the sequels.  Dark Empire looks good and makes sense compared to the sequels Ride of Skywalker.

So, no, they're not going to see the same level of cultural acceptance or nostalgia.  They're just bad movies.
